preFix.title: Four-shaft undulating twill scarf
preFix.subtitle: A 200 × 30 cm scarf in 2/16 wool, woven with an undulating treadling sequence on four shafts
preFix.excerpt: Undulating twill uses a standard twill threading with a varied treadling that doubles back on itself, bending the diagonal lines into S-shaped waves across the cloth. Worked in 2/16 wool, the scarf has a dense, lofty hand that benefits from a firm wet finish.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard four-shaft twill weaving references and public-domain weave structure documentation.

content.text: Before the draft, here is a plain-English description of the structure. Standard
steps the diagonal in one direction for every pick: 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 3, 4. Undulating twill (sometimes called wavy twill) uses the same

that reverses partway through the sequence: 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1. The doubling-back bends the diagonal line into an S-shape or wave across the width of the cloth. The effect is subtle on small cloth and more pronounced on a wide warp or with contrasting warp and weft colours.
This scarf finishes at approximately 200 × 30 cm in 2/16 wool,
at 18 EPI (7 ends/cm). Allow 10% take-up in the width and 10% shrinkage in the length after
. Warp to approximately 260 cm (including loom waste) at a width of 33 cm in the reed. Total warp ends: 33 cm × 7 ends/cm = approximately 231 ends. Use matching
colours for a woven-back effect, or two contrasting colours to maximise the wave line visibility.

items.name: Four-shaft floor loom with at least 35 cm weaving width

content.text: Reading the draft: the threading is a straight 1-2-3-4 draw across the full warp width. The tie-up is a standard balanced 2/2 twill: treadle 1 lifts shafts 1 and 2; treadle 2 lifts shafts 2 and 3; treadle 3 lifts shafts 3 and 4; treadle 4 lifts shafts 4 and 1. The treadling sequence is 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1 repeating. The repeated treadle in the middle of each sequence (the two consecutive 4s and two consecutive 1s at the join of each repeat) creates the reversal point: the apex and nadir of the wave.
Wind 231 ends on the warping board to 260 cm. Beam under firm, even tension. Thread each end through the
on the correct shaft in the 1-2-3-4 straight draw. Sley the 9-dent reed at 2 ends per dent throughout to achieve 18 EPI. Tie onto the front apron rod in small, even bouts.
Weave a 4 cm header of plain weave to spread the warp to the full width. Check
tension: both edges should have the same resistance and the weft should loop cleanly around the outermost end on each side.
with treadle 1 (shafts 1 and 2 rise). Pass the shuttle through. Beat to a balanced
count: approximately 7 picks per cm to match the sett.

Follow the treadling sequence: 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1. Then repeat from 1. Keep a tally of each pick to avoid losing your place in the sequence. The wave pattern becomes visible after 3 to 4 full repeats (24 to 32 picks). The

length at the reversal points is 2 threads, which is acceptable for a scarf at this sett. Continue weaving until the on-loom length reaches approximately 222 cm (to finish at 200 cm after 10% shrinkage).

Weave 4 cm of plain weave at each end and work a hemstitch in groups of 4 warp ends before cutting from the loom. Trim fringe to a consistent 5 cm, or knot in groups of 4 ends close to the hemstitched edge and trim the fringe close to the knot.

Wet finish by hand-washing in warm water (30 °C) with a wool wash product. Submerge without agitating, soak for 5 minutes, then rinse at the same temperature. Roll in a towel to remove excess water. Lay flat and press firmly with a warm iron (wool setting) while still damp. The undulating twill lines will sharpen and the cloth will relax to its finished width.

items.solution: The treadling sequence may have been pressed in the wrong order, eliminating the reversal. Check that the sequence includes the double treadle at the reversal point (two consecutive 4s and two consecutive 1s). Also try contrasting warp and weft colours for a more readable result.

items.problem: Long floats appearing at the reversal points
items.solution: At the treadling reversal, treadle 4 is pressed twice in a row. This is by design and creates a float of 2 threads, which is correct for this structure. Floats longer than 2 threads indicate a sequence error.

items.problem: Selvedges pulling in on one side
items.solution: Check that the weft is being carried at the same arc on both passes. The shuttle direction alternates on each pick; make sure the arc is generous in both directions.

items.problem: Scarf is too stiff after wet finishing
items.solution: The beat may be too heavy, packing the picks too tightly at 18 EPI. Ease the beat for the remaining length. A heavy beat on 2/16 wool produces a firm, blanket-like cloth rather than a scarf-weight drape.
